source_policy.pdf_rule: Only import PDF cards from trusted local extraction evidence; otherwise store blocked or unproven segment metadata without generated cards.

question_styles.label: Conceptual recall
question_styles.prompt_template: Before opening {title}, explain the core idea behind {pattern}. What invariant, decision rule, or observable cue tells you this concept is relevant?
question_styles.answer_guidance: Use this as a self-check for recalling the concept, not a source solution. Name the invariant, the cue that activates it, and one competing idea you would rule out.

question_styles.label: Application scenario
question_styles.prompt_template: Turn {title} into a realistic {difficulty} interview scenario. How would you apply {pattern} when constraints, data shape, or production tradeoffs change?
question_styles.answer_guidance: Self-grade by explaining the adapted approach, the assumption that matters most, and what would change if input size, latency, memory, or data quality were different.

question_styles.label: Debugging and edge cases
question_styles.prompt_template: For {title}, list the boundary cases and likely failure modes before submitting. Include empty, minimal, duplicate, ordering, and constraint-boundary cases when they apply.
question_styles.answer_guidance: Compare your tests against the bugs you actually hit. Record concrete examples in your editable notes; no copied problem statement or solution text is imported.

question_styles.label: Complexity and implementation traps
question_styles.prompt_template: State the time and space complexity for your {pattern} approach to {title}, then name the implementation traps: off-by-one risks, state invariants, mutation hazards, duplicate handling, and language-specific pitfalls.
question_styles.answer_guidance: Self-grade by naming the dominant operation or state count, the data structure choice controlling the bound, and the invariant that prevents the most likely bug.
